All job steps verified green locally with the exact CI commands: go build ./..., go test ./... , gofmt -l (clean), go vet ./..., golangci-lint run (0 issues). Literal 'CI green on a PR' is observed on first push to the remote (no infra changes the outcome — same Go toolchain).

phases.name: pull / hydrate for Postgres (deterministic, own synthesis engine)
phases.ships: `pull` (fast mode) and `hydrate` for Postgres. Reads production shape read-only, emits a committable rowshape.yaml, and reconstructs a deterministic disposable database from it.
phases.scope_note: Fast mode only: catalog + pg_stats + reservoir sample. HyperLogLog, uniqueness existence probes, auto-escalation, and `inspect --leaks` are DEFERRED to phase-1b. In phase 1, `distinct` stays `estimated` (via pg_stats) and `unique` is `exact` only when a catalog constraint/index proves it, otherwise absent (INV-UNIQUENESS).
definition_of_done: A read-only `pull` against a Postgres produces a valid, digest-stable, <100KB rowshape.yaml in fast mode, with a confidence attached to every fact and privacy enforced.
`hydrate --seed N` deterministically reconstructs a disposable database honoring null_fraction (+/-0.5%), `unique`, and fan-out distribution shape.
Declared rows and hydrated rows are never conflated (RFC §9).
Week-6 kill-criteria smoke passes: pull + hydrate reproduces a known migration pathology (kill_criteria Week 6).

tasks.title: Fixture Go data model (internal/fixture)
tasks.description: Model the entire RFC §5/§6 document as Go structs: meta (id, generated_at, generator, engine{name,version}, privacy, source, profile{mode,scanned_at,escalated}, digest); tables map keyed by qualified name; column profiles (universal §6.1, numeric/temporal §6.2, format classes §6.3); constraints §6.4; indexes §6.5; references/fanout §6.6. Scalar facts are {value, confidence, via} objects, NOT bare scalars. Readers MAY accept a bare scalar as shorthand for confidence:estimated (weakest reading). Unknown fields ignored, x_ vendor extensions preserved, unknown major version refused (RFC §12).
acceptance_criteria: Structs round-trip to/from the YAML examples in RFC §5 and §6 byte-for-byte (modulo canonicalization).
A bare scalar deserializes as {value, confidence: estimated} (RFC §6.1).
`tables` is a map keyed by qualified name, not a list (RFC §5).
Unknown fields are ignored; unknown major `rowshape_fixture` version is refused (RFC §12).
Confidence type enforces ordering exact > measured > estimated > declared (RFC §7.1).
verification: go test ./internal/fixture/... (round-trip + confidence-ordering tests)

tasks.title: Canonical form + digest (single Go implementation)
tasks.description: Implement RFC §11 canonical form: keys sorted lexicographically, two-space indent, no anchors/aliases, floats to 6 significant figures, \n line endings, meta.digest and meta.generated_at excluded. Compute meta.digest = SHA-256 over the canonical form. This is the ONE canonical implementation the phase-5 API will import (INV-ONE-CANONICAL-FORM).
acceptance_criteria: Two canonicalizations of the same fixture are byte-identical.
Digest is stable across runs against an unchanged database and across platforms (RFC §13).
Floats render to exactly 6 significant figures; keys are lexicographically sorted; meta.digest and meta.generated_at are excluded from the hashed bytes.
verification: go test ./internal/fixture/... (golden canonical-form + digest-stability tests, run on linux+darwin+windows in CI)

tasks.title: `pull` — read structure via catalog (read-only)
tasks.description: Implement schema reads via catalog queries only: tables, columns, types, structural nullability, constraints (§6.4), indexes (§6.5), references + on_delete (§6.6). No SELECT * on user tables. Require a read-only role; refuse to run as superuser without --i-know. An existing unique constraint/index is a proof of uniqueness -> set unique {value:true, confidence:exact, via:constraint} for free. Preserve validated:false (NOT VALID) constraints.
acceptance_criteria: Produces the tables map with constraints, indexes, and references populated from the catalog.
Unique constraint/index sets unique.confidence=exact via constraint (RFC §6.4).
validated:false constraints are preserved (RFC §6.4).
Refuses to run as superuser without --i-know; uses a read-only path (no writes, no SELECT * on user tables).
The connection URL and any credentials are never logged, persisted, or written into the fixture (PRD §5, Priya is the buyer).
verification: go test against a testcontainers Postgres with a seeded schema
assert superuser refusal without --i-know

tasks.title: `pull` fast-mode column profiling (pg_stats + reservoir sample)
tasks.description: Profile each column in fast mode: null_fraction, distinct (estimated via pg_stats), numeric/temporal range + optional histogram (§6.2), text length stats, and format-class inference from a sample against the closed vocabulary (§6.3) with `opaque` as mandatory fallback. MUST NOT emit range/min/max on text or bytea columns (§6.1) — only length. Most facts land at `estimated`. Uniqueness is NOT inferred here (that is catalog-proven in P1-T3 or deferred to phase-1b probes); never infer unique:true from the sample (INV-UNIQUENESS).
acceptance_criteria: Every emitted fact carries a confidence; sample-derived facts are `estimated`.
text and bytea columns NEVER receive range/min/max (only length) — asserted by test (RFC §6.1 MUST).
format is one of the closed §6.3 vocabulary; ambiguous columns emit `opaque`.
jsonb_shape carries a key skeleton (names/depth/leaf types) but never leaf values (§6.3).
No unique:true is produced from the sample.
verification: go test ./internal/profile/... (text/bytea range-omission, format-class, jsonb skeleton, confidence-tagging)

tasks.title: Privacy levels + redaction in the emitter
tasks.description: Enforce RFC §8 at emit time. strict / standard (default) / permissive. strict emits none of: numeric/temporal range, histograms, values/frequencies, verbatim CHECK expressions (they become opaque). permissive materializes values/frequencies only when distinct<=50 AND every value occurs >= k times (default k=20). Per-column overrides (redact: [range, histogram] | all) always win. MUST support strict; MUST NOT default to permissive. meta.source is a salted per-fixture hash of the host, never the hostname (§8.4).
acceptance_criteria: strict output contains no range, histogram, values/frequencies, or verbatim CHECK expressions.
default privacy is `standard`; permissive is never the default.
permissive value sets appear only when distinct<=50 AND min per-value count >= k (k=20 default).
per-column `redact` overrides are honored.
meta.source is a salted hash, never the hostname.
verification: go test ./internal/profile/... (per-level field matrix from RFC §8.2, k-threshold, redact overrides, source-hash)

tasks.title: Emit rowshape.yaml (assembled fixture)
tasks.description: Assemble the profiled data into a rowshape.yaml with a complete meta block: id, generated_at, generator, engine{name,version} (version MANDATORY — RFC §9.1), privacy, source (salted hash), profile{mode:fast, scanned_at, escalated:[]}, and computed digest. Write human-readable, diffable YAML. `rowshape init` should scaffold config; wire the minimal `init` here.
acceptance_criteria: Output is valid YAML that round-trips through the P1-T1 data model.
meta.engine.version is always present.
Fixture for a 200-table schema is under 100KB (RFC §3.3).
meta.digest matches a fresh recomputation over the file.
verification: rowshape pull --from $TEST_URL -> rowshape.yaml; rowshape (re)digest matches; yaml lint; size check on a synthetic 200-table schema

tasks.title: `hydrate` — own deterministic synthesis engine
tasks.description: Build the synthesis engine (NOT gofakeit — PRD §16). Given rowshape.yaml + --seed, synthesize N rows into a disposable database. Determinism per RFC §10: per-column PRNG via hash(seed, table, column, row_ordinal) so adding a column or changing --scale does not reshuffle other columns' prefixes; generation order MUST NOT depend on Go map iteration order (canonicalize first). Honor null_fraction within +/-0.5%, honor `unique`, reproduce fan-out DISTRIBUTION shape (p50/p95/max), not just the mean (§6.6); satisfy declared constraints unless orphan_fraction>0 demands otherwise. Substitute obviously-fake values (e.g. user_00417@example.invalid) — realism of shape, not content (RFC §13).
acceptance_criteria: hydrate --seed N is byte-reproducible for the same fixture + hydrator version across platforms.
null_fraction reproduced within +/-0.5%; `unique` honored.
fan-out distribution shape reproduced (p50/p95/max), not just the mean.
Generated values are obviously fake; content realism is explicitly NOT attempted.
Changing --scale does not reshuffle the retained prefix of values.
verification: go test ./internal/hydrate/... (determinism across platforms in CI matrix, null_fraction tolerance, fan-out distribution assertion, scale-prefix stability)

tasks.title: Declared rows vs. hydrated rows separation
tasks.description: Guarantee RFC §9: the fixture always declares production rows (e.g. 1.2M) even when hydrate --scale 0.01 materializes 12k. The data model and hydrate path must keep declared rows (drives later extrapolation) strictly separate from the hydrated count (tests correctness). They MUST NOT be conflated anywhere.
acceptance_criteria: hydrate --scale 0.01 on a fixture declaring 1.2M rows creates ~12k rows while the fixture still reports declared rows = 1.2M.
No code path uses hydrated count where declared rows are meant (guarded by test).
verification: go test ./internal/hydrate/... (scale test asserting declared vs materialized counts diverge correctly)

tasks.title: Disposable hydrate target orchestration
tasks.description: Provide the disposable Postgres target for hydrate: default via testcontainers-go (Docker), and accept a user-provided --target URL. Records open-question 17.2 (testcontainers vs embedded/pg_tmp); ship testcontainers-go as the default now and leave the swap point clean. The container is thrown away after use.
acceptance_criteria: hydrate spins up a disposable Postgres (testcontainers-go default) and tears it down after.
--target <url> hydrates into a provided database instead.
The target abstraction is a single interface so pg_tmp/embedded can be swapped without touching the engine.
verification: go test ./internal/target/... (container lifecycle up/teardown; provided-URL path)

tasks.title: Week-6 gate: reproduce a known pathology end-to-end
tasks.description: Kill-criteria checkpoint (Week 6). Using a small handcrafted pathology fixture (a precursor to the full phase-2 corpus) — e.g. `email` at 3.2% null and a fan-out column with a long tail (max >> mean) — prove pull->rowshape.yaml->hydrate produces a database where a direct query confirms the null ratio and the fan-out tail actually exist. If this fails, STOP and surface: the fixture premise is wrong and `validate` must not be built on it.
acceptance_criteria: A checked-in pathology fixture hydrates into a DB where querying confirms null_fraction is within tolerance of 3.2%.
The fan-out tail is reproduced: measured max fan-out >> mean, matching the fixture's p95/max within tolerance.
This runs in CI as an executable smoke test.
verification: go test ./test/pathology/... (hydrate then SQL-assert null ratio and fan-out tail)

tasks.title: Fan-out + orphan_fraction measurement (the moat field)
tasks.description: Measure the reference fan-out distribution and orphan_fraction (RFC §6.6). The catalog (P1-T3) gives the FK edges and on_delete, but the DISTRIBUTION — children per parent as {mean, p50, p95, max} — and orphan_fraction must be measured by aggregate queries over data, not read from the catalog and not inferred from column stats. In fast mode fan-out is estimated from a sample; orphan_fraction reaches `exact` via a scan (aligns with the phase-1b probes). This is the field no other tool captures and what turns a cascade delete into an outage (RFC §6.6).
acceptance_criteria: Each FK reference carries a fanout distribution {mean, p50, p95, max} with a confidence (estimated in fast mode, measured on a full pass).
orphan_fraction is populated (exact via scan), and nonzero orphans are recorded so a VALIDATE finding can consume them (RFC §6.6, RS-DATA).
Fan-out is measured by aggregation over FK relationships, never inferred from column stats or the catalog alone.
verification: go test against a seeded skewed-fanout DB: p95/max recovered within tolerance; orphan_fraction exact on a DB with known orphans

tasks.title: Skew-aware histograms + partitioned-table shape
tasks.description: Make histograms first-class for skewed columns and capture partitioned-table shape. RFC §6.2: a tenant_id where one tenant owns 80% of rows behaves completely differently under a partitioning migration, and no summary statistic captures that skew — so emit the histogram (standard+ privacy) and reproduce the skew in hydrate, not just the mean/range. RFC §14.2 (leaning): a partitioned table declares a `partitions: {count, strategy, skew}` block with no per-partition entries, so a partitioning migration is reasoned about from the fixture.
spec_refs: RFC §14.2 (partitioned tables: parent declares partitions block)
acceptance_criteria: Skewed columns emit a histogram (bounds/buckets) at privacy standard+; hydrate reproduces the skew, not just the mean/range.
Partitioned tables emit a `partitions: {count, strategy, skew}` block (no per-partition entries).
Histogram bounds honor privacy — dropped under strict (RFC §8.2), since bounds are literally real values.
verification: go test: hydrated distribution reproduces an 80/20 skew within tolerance; a partitioned source emits the partitions block; strict drops histogram bounds

phases.name: Confidence model — HLL, uniqueness probes, auto-escalation, inspect --leaks
phases.ships: The confidence engine: client-side HyperLogLog, exact uniqueness probes, auto-escalation of dangerous columns, and `inspect --leaks`. This is what makes a cheap `fast` fixture safe by construction.
phases.scope_note: This phase upgrades facts from `estimated` toward `measured`/`exact` and adds the self-audit surface. It does not build findings or capping yet (those consume this in phase 2), but the correctness of capping in phase 2 rests entirely on the confidence levels produced here.
definition_of_done: distinct can reach `measured` via client-side HLL with a published error rate.
unique reaches `exact` only via catalog, existence probe, or count comparison — never a sample (INV-UNIQUENESS).
fast mode auto-escalates exactly the dangerous columns (n_distinct/rows > 0.95, no unique constraint), with a cost ceiling that WARNs on what it skips.
`rowshape inspect --leaks` enumerates every value-derived field with its source column and privacy level.

tasks.title: Client-side HyperLogLog distinct estimation
tasks.description: Stream each column with a server-side cursor, hash each value, discard it — bounded memory, no values retained, no server extension (postgresql-hll is unavailable on most managed Postgres, so depending on it would gate the feature behind the infra that most needs it). Sets distinct {confidence: measured, via: hll, error: e}. Precision 14 (~1.6% error, 16KB state) per open-question RFC §14.4 leaning; the error rate is published in distinct.error and feeds §7.4.
acceptance_criteria: distinct via HLL lands at confidence `measured` with `error` populated.
No column values are retained in memory beyond the streaming hash.
Runs without any server-side extension.
Measured error stays within the precision-14 bound on a known-cardinality test column.
verification: go test ./internal/profile/hll/... (accuracy within bound; memory bounded; no-extension path)

tasks.title: Exact uniqueness probes (existence + count)
tasks.description: Implement the two probe-based routes to unique=exact from RFC §7.2: (2) existence probe `SELECT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM t WHERE c IS NOT NULL GROUP BY c HAVING count(*)>1)` — one boolean out, short-circuits on first duplicate; (3) count comparison `SELECT count(*)-count(DISTINCT c) FROM t` — one integer out, gives duplicate count. Route (1), catalog proof, already lands free in P1-T3. If none is affordable, omit `unique` entirely. NEVER infer unique from a sample.
acceptance_criteria: unique reaches `exact` only via constraint, existence probe, or count comparison.
Probes emit no row values (boolean/integer only).
When no route is affordable, `unique` is omitted, not guessed.
A property test confirms unique:true is never produced from sampling.
verification: go test ./internal/profile/... (probe correctness on unique + non-unique columns; omission path)

tasks.title: Auto-escalation of dangerous columns (default behavior)
tasks.description: In fast mode, automatically escalate to a full pass any column where n_distinct/rows > 0.95 AND no unique constraint exists — exactly the columns that look unique but aren't proven, where a wrong answer costs an outage. Record escalated columns in meta.profile.escalated and set mode `targeted` when any escalation fires. This makes the cheap default safe by construction, not by remembering a flag.
acceptance_criteria: Columns matching the predicate are escalated automatically; others are not.
meta.profile.escalated lists exactly the escalated columns.
mode becomes `targeted` when escalation occurs, `fast` otherwise.
verification: go test ./internal/profile/... (escalation predicate selects the right column set; escalated list + mode recorded)

tasks.title: Escalation cost ceiling (soft cap + WARN)
tasks.description: Resolve open-question PRD §17.3 / RFC §14.5: a fast pull that quietly full-scans a 400M-row table is a bad surprise. Implement a soft cap (--max-escalation-rows) that, when exceeded, degrades to omitting `unique` for that column and emits a WARN naming exactly what was skipped and why. Silent truncation is forbidden.
acceptance_criteria: Escalation beyond the cap omits `unique` (safe per §7.4) rather than blocking.
A WARN names the skipped column and the row count that triggered the cap.
Default cap is documented; overridable via --max-escalation-rows.
verification: go test ./internal/profile/... (cap triggers omission + WARN; no silent skip)

tasks.title: `exact` profile mode (full streaming pass)
tasks.description: Implement the `exact` profile mode from RFC §7.3: a full streaming pass per table — HLL for distinct, exact null counts, exact uniqueness probes. Minutes to hours; reads values into emitter memory but emits only aggregates. This is the manual counterpart to auto-escalation for the whole database.
acceptance_criteria: `rowshape pull --exact` performs a full pass; null counts are `exact`, distinct is `measured` via HLL.
Only aggregates are emitted; no row values leave the emitter.
meta.profile.mode records `exact`.
verification: go test ./internal/profile/... (exact-mode confidence levels; aggregate-only output)

tasks.title: `rowshape inspect --leaks`
tasks.description: Enumerate every field in a given fixture derived from row values, with its source column and privacy level (RFC §8.3). A trust feature, not a compliance checkbox: Priya's security team finds these fields anyway; pointing at them first is the difference between a documented tradeoff and an undisclosed leak. Ship in v1 (PRD §11).
acceptance_criteria: Lists every value-derived field (range, histogram bounds, values/frequencies, CHECK expressions, length.max on free_text) with source column + privacy level.
Output is complete: a test fixture with known leaks enumerates all of them and nothing spurious.
Exits non-zero option available for CI gating (documented).
verification: go test ./cmd/... (inspect --leaks on a fixture with seeded value-derived fields lists exactly them)

phases.name: Corpus + capping tests FIRST, then validate + verdict contract + RS-LOCK / RS-DATA
phases.ships: The corpus and capping regression suite, then `validate`, the verdict contract, and the first finding classes (RS-LOCK, RS-DATA, RS-CONSTRAINT, RS-INDEX) with model-based extrapolation across the PG version matrix.
phases.ordering_rule: The corpus (P2-T1) and the capping tests (P2-T2) land BEFORE the findings they test (P2-T8..T11). Reversing this means writing findings, demoing them, then discovering which were wrong on PG 11 (PRD §14, §12).
definition_of_done: rowshape/corpus runs (migration, fixture, expected verdict) triples against every supported PG major (11-17).
Capping tests exist and pass BEFORE the findings, asserting RFC §7.4 (estimated -> WARN, unproven uniqueness cannot PASS).
One Verdict struct with two marshalers renders identically for CLI, MCP, and Action; exit codes 0/1/2/3 honored; verdict is DSSE-signable shaped.

validate hydrates a disposable target, applies the migration through the detected runner, and returns RS-LOCK / RS-DATA / RS-CONSTRAINT / RS-INDEX findings with bucketed, version-conditional estimates and mandatory remediation.

tasks.title: Corpus repo scaffold (rowshape/corpus)
tasks.description: Create rowshape/corpus: executable (migration, fixture, expected verdict) triples covering the documented ways Postgres migrations break — volatile default rewrite, SET NOT NULL full-scan, unique index that can't build, VALIDATE tripping on pre-existing orphans, cascade delete fan-out tail, NOT VALID constraint validated in the same transaction. A runnable harness, not prose. This is an asset people star and cite (PRD §12).
acceptance_criteria: Repo holds at least one triple per pathology named in PRD §12.
Each triple is executable via a test harness that consumes a fixture + migration and asserts the expected verdict.
Harness is parameterized by PG major version (wired to the matrix in P2-T13).
verification: corpus test harness runs green on the seeded triples (against PG 16 initially)

tasks.title: Capping tests FIRST (the wrong-PASS regression suite)
tasks.description: Before any finding exists, write corpus entries that exercise RFC §7.4 capping: a fixture profiling a column at `estimated` cannot yield PASS for a finding depending on it (must WARN naming the resolving command); uniqueness that is unproven cannot certify ADD CONSTRAINT UNIQUE. These are untested assertions until something exercises them — and a wrong PASS is unrecoverable (PRD §11, §15). Write these before the findings they cap.
acceptance_criteria: A triple where the relevant fact is `estimated` asserts a WARN (not PASS) with the resolving command named.
A triple with unproven uniqueness asserts ADD CONSTRAINT UNIQUE cannot PASS.
These tests are committed and RED (pending the capping engine P2-T4) — they define the contract findings must satisfy.
verification: corpus capping suite present; goes GREEN only once P2-T4 lands

tasks.title: Verdict struct + two marshalers (DSSE-signable shape)
tasks.description: Implement the one `Verdict` struct in internal/verdict matching PRD §10 exactly: rowshape, verdict, fixture{id,digest}, duration_ms, findings[] with code/severity/title/location/detail/evidence/estimate/depends_on/confidence/remediation/explain. Two marshalers: JSON and a human renderer that is a RENDERING of the struct, never a separate code path. Exit codes 0 PASS / 1 FAIL / 2 WARN-only / 3 tool error. Shape it as a DSSE/in-toto predicate from day one (§9.1): subject = fixture digest + migration file digests; predicateType = https://rowshape.com/attestation/v1.
acceptance_criteria: JSON output matches the PRD §10 example schema (field-for-field).
Human output is produced by rendering the same struct (proven by a test that both marshalers consume one Verdict value).
Exit codes map 0/1/2/3 as specified; WARN-only is configurable to fail.
Verdict serializes as a DSSE predicate with subject = fixture digest + migration file digests; predicateType is the owned URI.
remediation is required (non-empty) on every finding with severity error.
verification: go test ./internal/verdict/... (JSON golden vs PRD §10; single-struct/two-marshaler test; exit-code table; DSSE predicate shape; remediation-required)

tasks.title: Confidence capping engine
tasks.description: Each finding declares `depends_on` (fixture facts). The verdict a finding can produce is capped by the MINIMUM confidence across its dependencies: exact/measured -> PASS allowed; estimated/declared/absent -> WARN max. A capped finding produces a WARN naming the command that resolves it. Findings MUST NOT downgrade a dependency's confidence to reach a stronger verdict — enforce this in code, not by convention. Turns P2-T2 GREEN.
acceptance_criteria: Verdict severity is capped at the min confidence of the finding's declared dependencies.
estimated/absent dependency -> WARN with a resolving command string.
No API allows lowering a dependency's confidence to raise a verdict (structurally prevented).
The P2-T2 capping corpus suite passes.
verification: go test ./internal/verdict/... + corpus capping suite green

tasks.title: Model-based, version-conditional extrapolation
tasks.description: Extrapolate cost/duration per operation class along a known model (table rewrite O(n), constraint validation O(n), b-tree build O(n log n), CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY O(n log n) two-pass no exclusive lock, catalog-only O(1)) using DECLARED rows, not hydrated count. Cost models are engine-version-conditional (PG11+ catalog fast-path for non-volatile defaults; volatile default still rewrites; SET NOT NULL still full-scans). MUST refuse to extrapolate when engine.version is absent. Report buckets (instant/fast/noticeable/slow/outage) with basis attached; estimate confidence never exceeds the confidence of `rows`.
acceptance_criteria: Each operation class maps to its model; extrapolation uses declared rows.
Refuses to extrapolate (and says so) when engine.version is missing.
Findings report buckets with {model, basis_rows, basis_ms, declared_rows}; never a point estimate.
A version-conditional case differs correctly between PG 11 and PG 16 (asserted in corpus).
verification: go test ./internal/estimate/... (model table; version-conditional divergence; no-version refusal; bucket output)

tasks.title: Runner detection (Alembic, Prisma, Drizzle, raw SQL)
tasks.description: Detect the user's migration runner and shell out to it — orchestrate, do not reimplement. Support Alembic, Prisma, Drizzle, and plain raw-SQL directories (psql -f). Rowshape applies the migration set through the detected runner against the disposable target only.
acceptance_criteria: Auto-detects Alembic / Prisma / Drizzle / raw-SQL dir from the repo.
Shells out to the detected tool; no migration logic is reimplemented.
Detection is overridable/explicitly selectable.
verification: go test ./internal/runner/... (detection fixtures per tool; shell-out invocation)

tasks.title: `validate` orchestration (the main event)
tasks.description: Wire `rowshape validate [--json]`: hydrate a disposable Postgres (from P1-T9), apply the current migration set via the detected runner (P2-T6), and capture success/failure, per-statement wall time, lock mode + duration, rows affected, constraint violations, and index build behavior. Feed captures into findings + extrapolation + capping and emit the Verdict. HARD-REFUSE if the target host matches the fixture source host; never touch a non-disposable DB.
acceptance_criteria: validate hydrates, applies, captures the six signal classes, and returns a Verdict.
--json emits the machine verdict; default emits the human rendering of the same struct.
Refuses when target host == fixture source host; only operates on disposable/provided targets.
validate makes no network call to any cloud service.
When run against a provided live branch/target (e.g. --target $NEON_BRANCH_URL), the relevant facts are upgraded to `exact` (PRD §15, branching complementarity).
verification: go test ./cmd/... (end-to-end validate on a corpus triple); assert host-match refusal; assert no cloud egress

tasks.title: RS-LOCK findings (lock mode + rewrite duration)
tasks.description: Detect lock-mode and table-rewrite pathologies: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE locks, volatile-default rewrites, type changes. Report lock_mode, rows_rewritten, bucketed duration via the extrapolation engine, and mandatory expand/backfill/contract remediation. Version-conditional (consume engine.version). Corpus entry precedes/accompanies this per ordering rule.
acceptance_criteria: RS-LOCK-001 fires on the volatile-default rewrite corpus case with correct lock_mode + rows_rewritten evidence.
Duration is a bucket with basis; remediation is the split expand/backfill/contract recipe.
Finding declares depends_on (e.g. public.<t>.rows) and carries capped confidence.
Behaves version-conditionally between PG 11 and PG 16.
verification: corpus RS-LOCK cases green across PG matrix

tasks.title: RS-DATA findings (uniqueness, null-on-NOT-NULL, orphans)
tasks.description: Detect data-shape pathologies: ADD CONSTRAINT UNIQUE on a column whose uniqueness is unproven (must WARN via capping, e.g. RS-DATA-014), SET NOT NULL / NOT NULL column where null_fraction > 0, FOREIGN KEY ... VALIDATE against nonzero orphan_fraction. Every finding declares depends_on and is capped.
acceptance_criteria: RS-DATA-014 warns (never PASS) when uniqueness is `estimated`/absent, naming `rowshape pull --exact <col>`.
A NOT NULL addition against null_fraction>0 is flagged.
A VALIDATE against orphan_fraction>0 is flagged as will-fail.
All RS-DATA findings carry depends_on + capped confidence.
verification: corpus RS-DATA cases green; capping suite (P2-T2) satisfied by RS-DATA-014

tasks.title: RS-CONSTRAINT findings
tasks.description: Detect constraint pathologies: adding/validating CHECK or FK constraints that conflict with existing data, NOT VALID constraint validated in the same transaction, constraint validation full-scan cost. Consume constraints (§6.4) and validated flags. Bucketed, capped, remediated.
acceptance_criteria: Flags NOT VALID + VALIDATE in the same transaction.
Flags constraint additions that conflict with profiled data shape.
Reports validation scan cost as a bucket; remediation mandatory.
verification: corpus RS-CONSTRAINT cases green across matrix

tasks.title: RS-INDEX findings
tasks.description: Detect index pathologies: CREATE INDEX (non-concurrent) taking an exclusive lock, unique index that can't build on non-unique data, rebuild time driven by index bytes/bloat_estimate (§6.5). Recommend CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY where applicable. Bucketed via O(n log n) model, capped, remediated.
spec_refs: RFC §9.1 (b-tree O(n log n), CIC two-pass)
acceptance_criteria: Flags non-concurrent index builds that lock; recommends CONCURRENTLY.
Flags a unique index that cannot build given profiled uniqueness.
Rebuild duration bucket uses index bytes/bloat as basis.
verification: corpus RS-INDEX cases green across matrix

tasks.title: `rowshape explain <CODE>` (agent-readable remediation)
tasks.description: Implement `explain <CODE>` returning docs + remediation for a finding code, agent-readable, no web search required. Every error finding's `explain` field points here. remediation is mandatory (a finding an agent can't act on is a bug).
acceptance_criteria: explain RS-LOCK-001 (and every shipped code) returns structured remediation.
Content is data-driven from the same source the finding cites (no drift).
Every shipped finding code has an explain entry (test-enforced).
verification: go test ./cmd/... (explain covers every registered finding code)

tasks.title: PG version matrix in corpus CI (11-17)
tasks.description: Run the corpus (including capping and all finding classes) against every supported Postgres major, 11 through 17. A finding right on 16 can be wrong on 11; the matrix is what catches it (PRD §12). Extends the P0-T6 matrix-ready CI.
acceptance_criteria: CI runs the corpus against PG 11,12,13,14,15,16,17.
Version-conditional cases assert different verdicts per major where expected.
A regression on any major fails CI.
verification: CI matrix green across all seven majors

tasks.title: `validate --calibrate` (upgrade estimate to measured)
tasks.description: Implement --calibrate: hydrate at two scale factors, fit the actual cost curve, and upgrade the estimate confidence from `estimated` to `measured` for the migration you're genuinely nervous about. Slower and honest (RFC §9.2).
acceptance_criteria: --calibrate hydrates at two scales and fits the curve.
Calibrated estimate is marked confidence `measured` with basis from the two points.
Without --calibrate, estimates remain `estimated` (no silent upgrade).
verification: go test ./internal/estimate/... (two-point fit; confidence upgrade only under --calibrate)

tasks.title: `plan --against` and `verify --against` (read-only)
tasks.description: Implement `plan --against prod` (dry-run diff vs a live target) and `verify --against prod` (post-deploy: does reality match intent). verify is read-only by definition. Validating against a live branch/target should upgrade the relevant facts to `exact` (PRD §15, Neon complementarity). These are the non-mutating live-target surfaces from §8.1.
acceptance_criteria: plan --against produces a dry-run diff without applying anything.
verify --against performs read-only checks only (no writes, enforced).
Validating against a provided live target upgrades the relevant facts to `exact`.
verification: go test ./cmd/... (plan diff; verify read-only enforcement; fact-upgrade on live target)

tasks.title: Fixture-spec conformance suite + published JSON Schema
tasks.description: The spec's strategic value is that others can emit it (PRD §3, §16). Ship, in rowshape/fixture-spec: (1) a published machine-readable JSON Schema for rowshape.yaml (format version "1"), and (2) a conformance test suite encoding the three RFC §13 profiles — a conformant emitter (read-only, strict on demand, never `range` on text, never `unique` from a sample, confidence on every fact, stable digest), a conformant hydrator (null_fraction ±0.5%, honor `unique`, reproduce fanout shape, deterministic), and a conformant validator (cap per §7.4, buckets per §9.2, never downgrade confidence). rowshape's own CLI must pass it and a third party can run it against their emitter — this is what makes the spec a position rather than an aspiration.
acceptance_criteria: A JSON Schema for rowshape.yaml (format version "1") is published in the fixture-spec repo and validates the reference fixtures.
A conformance suite encodes the RFC §13 emitter/hydrator/validator MUSTs and is runnable by third parties.
rowshape's own pull/hydrate/validate pass the suite in CI, and the suite REJECTS a deliberately non-conformant fixture (range on text, unique-from-sample).
verification: conformance CI green against rowshape; suite rejects seeded non-conformant fixtures; JSON Schema validates all reference fixtures

tasks.title: Tool-error (exit 3) taxonomy — broken tool vs unsafe migration
tasks.description: An agent must never confuse 'the tool couldn't run' with 'the migration is unsafe.' Define and implement the exit-code-3 tool-error path for the operational failures that are neither PASS/FAIL/WARN: no Docker daemon / disposable target unavailable (PRD §17.2), can't connect to the target, runner not found or failed to invoke, fixture parse error, and an unknown fixture major version — which MUST refuse rather than best-effort (RFC §12). Each returns exit 3 with a structured, agent-readable reason clearly distinct from a verdict.
acceptance_criteria: All listed operational failures return exit code 3 with a structured reason, never a FAIL verdict.
An unknown fixture major version refuses with exit 3 (RFC §12) — never a partial-understanding PASS/FAIL.
The tool-error payload is machine-readable (an agent can branch on it) and its human rendering comes from the same shape.
verification: go test: each failure mode yields exit 3 + structured reason; unknown-major fixture refused; FAIL vs tool-error never conflated

phases.name: MCP subcommand + init --agent + the agent rule
phases.ships: `rowshape mcp` (the wedge): a subcommand of the same binary exposing four brutally-thin tools on the official Go SDK, plus `rowshape init` / `init --agent` which writes MCP config, the versioned agent rule, and a pre-commit backstop so an agent actually reaches for rowshape unprompted.
phases.scope_note: This phase closes the loop that the whole bet rests on (PRD §2, §15): getting rowshape into the agent's context and turn. The MCP tools are thin wrappers over surfaces already built in phase 2 (validate, explain, plan) and phase 1 (the fixture) — no new finding logic here. The differentiator is discipline: compact codes over fat schemas (MCPFold), and treating the agent rule as a versioned product artifact, not a README snippet.
definition_of_done: `rowshape init` detects the stack (engine + migration runner: Alembic, Prisma, Drizzle, raw-SQL dir) and scaffolds a committable config without touching a production database.
`rowshape mcp` serves the four tools (describe_shape, validate_migration, explain_finding, plan_against) over the official Go SDK as a subcommand of the single binary — not a separate artifact.
MCP schemas are thin: findings return compact codes with explain_finding as the only expansion path, and no tool returns a full fixture unless a specific table is requested (PRD §8.2).

`rowshape init --agent` writes MCP client config, a versioned agent rule into AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md / .cursor/rules, and a pre-commit hook running `rowshape validate` — all idempotent and re-runnable.
The agent rule ships versioned in the repo so improvements reach existing users on upgrade (PRD §8.3).

tasks.title: `rowshape init` — detect stack, scaffold config
tasks.description: Implement the base `rowshape init` from PRD §8.1: detect the database engine and the migration runner in use (Alembic, Prisma, Drizzle, or a plain SQL directory) and scaffold a committable rowshape config. Detection only — init never connects to a production database and never runs a migration. This is the foundation `init --agent` (P3-T8..T10) extends.
spec_refs: PRD §8 (runner detection: Alembic, Prisma, Drizzle, raw SQL)
acceptance_criteria: `rowshape init` detects the migration runner from repo layout (alembic.ini, prisma/schema.prisma, drizzle config, or a SQL migrations directory).
It writes a committable config file recording engine + detected runner; no network or database access occurs.
Re-running init is idempotent — it does not clobber user edits to the config.
verification: go test ./cmd/... (runner detection across Alembic/Prisma/Drizzle/raw-SQL fixtures; idempotent re-run; no DB connection attempted)

tasks.title: `rowshape mcp` subcommand scaffold on the official Go SDK
tasks.description: Wire `rowshape mcp` as a subcommand of the single binary (not a separate artifact) using modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk over stdio. Register the four tool handlers (filled by P3-T3..T6) and advertise `rowshape_fixture` major-version compatibility per RFC §12. This is the wedge surface from PRD §8.2; the SDK is past v1.0.0 with a no-breaking-changes guarantee (PRD §7).
spec_refs: PRD §7 (official Go MCP SDK, past v1.0.0)
acceptance_criteria: `rowshape mcp` starts a stdio MCP server built on modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk within the same binary.
The server registers exactly the four tools named in PRD §8.2 and no others.
Server initialization completes a handshake with a reference MCP client in a test.
verification: go test ./cmd/mcp/... (server boots, lists exactly four tools, completes initialize handshake against an in-process client)

tasks.title: MCP tool: describe_shape
tasks.description: Expose `describe_shape` — hand the agent the shape before it writes SQL (PRD §8.2). Reads a committed fixture and returns a compact view of a requested table's relevant facts (row count, null fractions, distinct/uniqueness confidence, fan-out, format classes). Never returns a full fixture unless a specific table is requested; the whole point is that the agent reasons over four kilobytes, not forty gigabytes (RFC §2).
spec_refs: PRD §8.2 (describe_shape; never return a full fixture unless asked for a table)
acceptance_criteria: describe_shape returns the shape for a named table, including confidence on each fact (RFC §7).
With no table specified it returns a table index/summary, not the full fixture body.
Output is compact — value-derived facts are summarized, honoring the fixture's privacy level (RFC §8).
verification: go test ./cmd/mcp/... (describe_shape returns per-table shape + confidence; refuses to dump full fixture without a table target)

tasks.title: MCP tool: validate_migration (the loop-closer)
tasks.description: Expose `validate_migration` — the loop-closer from PRD §8.2. Wraps the phase-2 `validate` orchestration and returns the Verdict as compact findings codes (RS-LOCK-001, etc.) plus the overall verdict and exit-code-equivalent, not prose. This is the tool that lets an agent write → validate → read the failure → fix → re-validate inside its own turn (PRD §2).
acceptance_criteria: validate_migration invokes the same validate path as the CLI and returns the same Verdict struct (one struct, PRD §10).
Findings are returned as compact codes + minimal fields, with `explain_finding` as the expansion path (not full remediation prose inline).
The tool surfaces the PASS/FAIL/WARN verdict and honors confidence capping (RFC §7.4) — an estimated-dependency finding never reports PASS.
verification: go test ./cmd/mcp/... (validate_migration returns the shared Verdict; compact codes; capping preserved end-to-end)

tasks.title: MCP tool: explain_finding
tasks.description: Expose `explain_finding` — remediation without a web search (PRD §8.2). Wraps `rowshape explain <CODE>` (P2-T12) so an agent that received a compact code from validate_migration can expand exactly the finding it hit. This is the deliberate expansion path that keeps validate_migration's schema thin (MCPFold discipline).
acceptance_criteria: explain_finding returns the same remediation content as the `rowshape explain <CODE>` CLI path.
Every error-severity finding code resolves to actionable remediation (a finding an agent can't act on is a bug, PRD §10).
An unknown code returns a clean tool error, not a crash.
verification: go test ./cmd/mcp/... (explain_finding matches CLI explain output; unknown code handled gracefully)

tasks.title: MCP tool: plan_against
tasks.description: Expose `plan_against` — what would change on target X (PRD §8.2). Wraps the phase-2 `plan --against` dry-run (P2-T15). Read-only: returns the diff a migration would produce against a live target without applying anything, and (per PRD §15) validating against a live target upgrades the relevant facts to `exact`.
acceptance_criteria: plan_against returns a dry-run diff against a provided target and applies nothing.
It reuses the CLI `plan --against` path (no reimplementation).
Read-only is enforced — the tool never issues a write against the target.
verification: go test ./cmd/mcp/... (plan_against returns dry-run diff; write-refusal enforced)

tasks.title: Thin-schema / MCPFold discipline + cross-link
tasks.description: Enforce the token-discipline PRD §8.2 demands: four fat tool schemas in every session costs tokens and earns resentment. Keep schemas brutally thin, return findings as compact codes with explain_finding as the expansion, and never return a full fixture unless a specific table is asked for. Add a schema-size guard test and cross-link MCPFold in both READMEs (rowshape as MCPFold's flagship consumer).
spec_refs: PRD §8.2 (MCPFold-shaped discipline; thin schemas; compact codes)
acceptance_criteria: A guard test asserts each tool's advertised input/output schema stays under a fixed token/character budget.
No tool returns a full fixture body by default; describe_shape requires a table target for detail.
README (CLI repo) and MCPFold README cross-link, naming rowshape as a flagship MCPFold consumer.
verification: go test ./cmd/mcp/... (schema-budget guard passes for all four tools)
manual: both READMEs cross-link

tasks.title: `init --agent` — write MCP client config
tasks.description: First of the three things `init --agent` writes (PRD §8.3): MCP config for the detected client — `.mcp.json`, or the client's own config path (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.). Detect the client from the repo/environment and register `rowshape mcp` as a server. An MCP server the agent never calls is decoration (PRD §8.3); this is what actually wires it in.
spec_refs: PRD §8.3 (init --agent item 1: MCP config for the detected client)
acceptance_criteria: `rowshape init --agent` writes a valid MCP config registering `rowshape mcp` for the detected client.
The correct path/format is chosen per client (.mcp.json or the client's own location).
Writing is idempotent — re-running merges rather than duplicating the server entry.
verification: go test ./cmd/... (MCP config written for each supported client; idempotent merge; valid JSON)

tasks.title: `init --agent` — the versioned agent rule
tasks.description: Second thing `init --agent` writes (PRD §8.3): an agent rule into AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md / .cursor/rules — before writing a migration call describe_shape; before opening a PR call validate_migration; never hand-wave a FAIL. The rule text is a product artifact iterated against real agent sessions like a prompt, and it ships versioned in the repo so improvements reach existing users on upgrade.
spec_refs: PRD §8.3 (init --agent item 2: agent rule; versioned; reaches existing users on upgrade)
acceptance_criteria: `init --agent` writes the rule into the detected agent-rule file(s) (AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md / .cursor/rules).
The rule text is stored versioned in the repo and carries a version marker so `init` can update it in place on upgrade.
Re-running updates an outdated managed block without clobbering surrounding user content.
verification: go test ./cmd/... (rule written to each target file; versioned managed-block update replaces old rule, preserves user text)

tasks.title: `init --agent` — pre-commit hook backstop
tasks.description: Third thing `init --agent` writes (PRD §8.3): a pre-commit hook running `rowshape validate` — the backstop for when the agent ignores the rule, which it sometimes will. Install a hook (or extend an existing pre-commit config) that blocks a commit on a FAIL verdict, so the guarantee doesn't depend solely on the agent honoring the rule.
spec_refs: PRD §8.3 (init --agent item 3: pre-commit hook running rowshape validate)
acceptance_criteria: `init --agent` installs a pre-commit hook that runs `rowshape validate` and blocks the commit on a FAIL.
It integrates with an existing pre-commit framework if one is present, else installs a native git hook.
Installation is idempotent and clearly reversible (documented uninstall).
verification: go test ./cmd/... (hook installed; blocks on FAIL verdict; idempotent; integrates with existing pre-commit config)

phases.name: GitHub Action, docs site (Cloudflare Pages), the agent-loop demo
phases.ships: The three launch surfaces: a GitHub Action that runs `validate` in anyone's CI and renders the shared Verdict, the docs site (Astro Starlight on Cloudflare Pages), and the end-to-end agent-loop demo that is the launch and the week-12 kill-criterion gate.
phases.scope_note: Nothing here adds finding logic or fixture facts — the Action and MCP tools all render the same Verdict struct built in phase 2 (PRD §10, one struct / two marshalers). This phase is distribution and proof: get `validate` into CI, publish honest docs (the narrow-and-true privacy claim, PRD §11/§8.1), and demonstrate the loop closes with no human turn. Per PRD §14.1, if the demo doesn't work end-to-end the wedge isn't real — this is a gate, not a polish pass.
definition_of_done: A GitHub Action runs `rowshape validate` in CI, renders the same Verdict struct as the CLI and MCP (PRD §10), honors exit codes 0/1/2/3, and annotates the PR with findings at file/line.
The docs site is live on Cloudflare Pages (Astro Starlight, zero JS by default) covering install, the finding catalog, and the narrow-and-true privacy claim from PRD §8.1/§11 verbatim.
The agent-loop demo runs end-to-end with no human turn: naive migration -> FAIL RS-LOCK-001 -> three-step backfill rewrite -> PASS (PRD §13).
A CI regression job asserts the demo still reaches PASS, so the wedge can't silently rot (PRD §14.1).

tasks.title: GitHub Action: run `validate` in CI
tasks.description: Ship a GitHub Action (thin wrapper over the signed binary from P0-T4, not a reimplementation) that runs `rowshape validate` in a consumer's CI. It renders the same Verdict struct as the CLI and MCP server — one struct, two marshalers (PRD §10) — and honors exit codes 0/1/2/3 so a FAIL fails the job and a WARN is configurable. This is the CI half of the agent loop from PRD §2.
spec_refs: PRD §8 (Action renders the same struct as CLI/MCP)
acceptance_criteria: The Action runs `rowshape validate` using the released binary and exits non-zero on a FAIL verdict.
WARN-only behavior is configurable (fail vs pass) per PRD §10 exit-code rules.
The Action consumes a committed fixture and a migration set without requiring a production credential in CI.
verification: Action integration test: green run on a PASS case, red run on a seeded FAIL case, configurable WARN

tasks.title: GitHub Action: PR annotations + check summary from the Verdict
tasks.description: Render findings into GitHub's PR surface: file/line annotations from each finding's `location` (PRD §10) and a check-run summary listing codes, severity, bucketed estimates, and remediation. Reuse the Verdict's fields directly — no separate formatting code path. This is what makes a FAIL loud in review before deploy (PRD §5, Dana's workflow).
acceptance_criteria: Each finding with a `location` produces a PR annotation at the correct file and line.
The check summary lists finding codes, severity, estimate bucket, and remediation.
Annotations are derived from the Verdict struct, not a bespoke formatter.
verification: Action integration test asserts annotations map finding.location -> file/line and summary contains codes + remediation

tasks.title: Docs site scaffold — Astro Starlight on Cloudflare Pages
tasks.description: Stand up the marketing + docs site per PRD §9: Astro Starlight, TypeScript, on Cloudflare Pages, shipping zero JS by default. This is explicitly the one place the stack is not Go — the Go decision was about the binary, not the website (PRD §9). Scaffold navigation, theme, and the deploy pipeline; content lands in P4-T4/T5.
spec_refs: PRD §9 (Astro Starlight on Cloudflare Pages, TypeScript, zero JS by default)
acceptance_criteria: An Astro Starlight site builds and deploys to Cloudflare Pages.
The default build ships zero client JS (Starlight default) and passes a link/build check in CI.
Navigation scaffolds sections for install, findings, privacy, MCP/agent, and the fixture spec.
verification: site build + deploy preview succeeds; CI asserts zero-JS default and no broken internal links

tasks.title: Docs content — install, finding catalog, the narrow-and-true privacy claim
tasks.description: Write the load-bearing docs: install/quickstart across the five distribution channels (P0-T4), the finding catalog (one page per RS-* code sourced from `explain`, P2-T12), and the privacy page using the exact narrow-and-true claim from PRD §8.1 — 'a fixture contains no rows from your database; it contains statistics computed from them; at standard some reveal extremes; at strict none do.' The broader claim is false and will be caught (PRD §11).
acceptance_criteria: The privacy page reproduces the PRD §8.1 defensible claim verbatim and does NOT make the broader 'no production values leave' claim.
Every shipped RS-* finding code has a catalog page whose remediation matches `rowshape explain <CODE>`.
Install docs cover all five distribution channels (brew, go install, npm wrapper, curl, Docker).
verification: docs build; a check asserts each `explain` code has a matching catalog page; privacy-claim wording lint

tasks.title: Docs content — MCP / init --agent guide + fixture-spec cross-link
tasks.description: Document the wedge: how to run `rowshape init --agent` and what it writes (MCP config, the versioned agent rule, the pre-commit hook — phase 3), and how the four MCP tools are used in an agent loop (PRD §8.2/§8.3). Cross-link the fixture-spec repo (P0-T2) and MCPFold, so the spec-as-position (PRD §3) is discoverable from the docs.
acceptance_criteria: A guide documents `init --agent` outputs and the agent-loop workflow with the four MCP tools.
The docs cross-link the fixture-spec repo and MCPFold.
The published agent rule text (P3-T9) is shown so users can see what gets written to CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md.
verification: docs build; link check confirms fixture-spec + MCPFold cross-links resolve

tasks.title: The agent-loop demo (the launch gate)
tasks.description: Build the demo that IS the launch (PRD §13): a Claude Code session, given a repo and a fixture, is told 'add a NOT NULL email column to users', produces a naive migration, watches it FAIL with RS-LOCK-001, rewrites it as a three-step backfill (ADD nullable; batched backfill; SET NOT NULL via validated CHECK), and reaches PASS — with no human turn in between. Assemble the demo repo, fixture, and the agent rule + MCP wiring that make the loop close unprompted.
spec_refs: PRD §13 (v1-done demo, record it before the README)
acceptance_criteria: A demo repo + committed fixture reproduce the naive migration -> FAIL RS-LOCK-001 path.
The rewrite to a three-step backfill reaches PASS.
The full loop runs with no human turn between the naive migration and the PASS, using the agent rule + MCP tools from phase 3.
verification: scripted end-to-end run: FAIL(RS-LOCK-001) then PASS on the rewrite, transcript captured

tasks.title: Demo regression job (protect the wedge)
tasks.description: Turn the demo into a regression guard so it can't silently rot: a CI job that runs the demo's naive migration and asserts FAIL RS-LOCK-001, then runs the three-step rewrite and asserts PASS. This makes the PRD §14.1 week-12 kill criterion continuously checkable instead of a one-time manual demo, and records the launch artifact deterministically.
spec_refs: PRD §15 (a single wrong PASS is unrecoverable)
acceptance_criteria: A CI job runs the demo and asserts FAIL RS-LOCK-001 on the naive migration and PASS on the rewrite.
The job runs on every push and blocks merge if the loop stops closing.
The demo run is deterministic (seeded hydrate) so the assertion is stable.
verification: CI: demo-regression job green (asserts both the FAIL and the PASS); deterministic across reruns

tasks.title: Agent-rule evaluation harness (measure the loop)
tasks.description: PRD §8.3 says the agent rule is 'iterated against real agent sessions the way a prompt does,' and PRD §15 says 'the bet is the loop, not the tool.' Build the harness that MEASURES whether the versioned rule (P3-T9) actually changes agent behavior: does the agent call describe_shape before writing SQL, call validate_migration before opening a PR, and stop hand-waving a FAIL? Run scripted agent sessions against the demo repo and score rule adherence and loop-closure rate, so rule changes are evaluated, not guessed.
spec_refs: PRD §8.3 (rule iterated like a prompt, versioned)
PRD §15 (the bet is the loop, not the tool)
acceptance_criteria: A harness runs scripted agent sessions and scores: describe_shape-before-SQL, validate-before-PR, and no-hand-waved-FAIL.
It reports a loop-closure rate (naive -> FAIL -> rewrite -> PASS with no human turn) as a tracked metric across rule versions.
A rule change can be A/B compared on these metrics before shipping (a deliberately weakened rule scores lower).
verification: harness runs the demo scenario end-to-end and emits adherence + closure metrics; a weakened rule scores measurably lower

phases.name: Findings breadth, version matrix depth, MySQL, then cloud only if pull-through is real
phases.ships: Everything after v1, in strict priority order: the remaining finding classes (RS-PERF, RS-REVERSE), deeper version-matrix coverage, MySQL as the second engine, and — only once the CLI has organic users — the commercial cloud (registry, audit, drift, attestation, billing).
phases.gating_rule: Cloud (P5-T7..T14) does not start until the traction gate passes (PRD §14, §14.1): without a few hundred stars and unsolicited issues/PRs, the cloud has no distribution and should not be built. Findings/matrix/MySQL (P5-T1..T6) are ungated post-v1 work; the cloud tasks are recorded now and built later, and only if pull-through is real.
definition_of_done: RS-PERF and RS-REVERSE finding classes exist, each landing its corpus triples BEFORE the finding (same capping discipline as phase 2, PRD §12/§14).
MySQL is a working second engine: the fixture spec vocabulary covers it (additive, RFC §12) and pull/hydrate/validate run against it (PRD §4).

If and only if the traction gate passes, the cloud ships: fixture registry, audit trail, approval gates, customer-side drift detection, and CI-OIDC-bound agent attestation — with `validate` never gated (PRD §8.4).
The cloud API imports the CLI's own `fixture` and `verdict` packages so canonical form and verdicts have exactly one implementation (PRD §9).

tasks.title: RS-PERF finding class (corpus-first)
tasks.description: Add the RS-PERF finding class from the permanent namespace in PRD §10. Query-plan/perf regression is explicitly OUT of v1 (PRD §13), so this is post-v1 breadth. Land the corpus triples that exercise the pathology BEFORE the finding, per the phase-2 ordering discipline (PRD §12, §14) — writing the finding first means discovering later which cases were wrong.
spec_refs: PRD §13 (query-plan regression is out of v1 -> phase 5)
acceptance_criteria: Corpus triples for the RS-PERF pathology land before the finding and assert the expected verdict.
RS-PERF findings carry depends_on + confidence and are capped per RFC §7.4.
Codes are permanent and namespaced (RS-PERF-NNN) per PRD §10.
verification: corpus harness green on RS-PERF cases across the PG matrix; capping preserved

tasks.title: RS-REVERSE finding class (reversibility, corpus-first)
tasks.description: Add the RS-REVERSE finding class (PRD §10) covering down-migration / reversibility hazards — e.g. a migration whose rollback loses data or cannot execute. Corpus triples first (PRD §12/§14). Findings declare their fixture dependencies and are capped by confidence like every other class (RFC §7.4).
acceptance_criteria: Corpus triples for reversibility hazards land before the finding.
RS-REVERSE findings carry mandatory remediation (PRD §10) and depends_on + confidence.
Codes are permanent and namespaced (RS-REVERSE-NNN).
verification: corpus harness green on RS-REVERSE cases; remediation present on every error

tasks.title: Version-matrix depth beyond the v1 capping cases
tasks.description: Deepen the version-conditional cost models (P2-T5) and expand corpus coverage across the full PG 11-17 matrix (P2-T13) beyond the v1 capping set — the documented cases where a finding right on 16 is wrong on 11 (PRD §12). A validator applying one table of models across all versions is confidently wrong on the older databases most likely to hold the scary migrations (RFC §9.1).
acceptance_criteria: New corpus triples cover version-divergent behaviors not in the v1 capping set, across PG 11-17.
Cost models consume engine.version and produce version-correct buckets on each major.
The matrix CI runs the expanded corpus on every supported major.
verification: corpus matrix CI green across PG 11-17 on the expanded case set

tasks.title: MySQL: extend the fixture-spec vocabulary
tasks.description: Extend the Rowshape Fixture Spec to cover MySQL. RFC §4 notes the structure anticipates other engines but the vocabulary does not yet cover them — so this adds MySQL engine facts additively (new fields, no reinterpretation) per the RFC §12 compatibility rules, keeping `rowshape_fixture: "1"` where possible. MySQL is the second engine (PRD §4).
spec_refs: RFC §4 (vocabulary does not yet cover other engines)
RFC §12 (additive fields ship without a major bump)
acceptance_criteria: The fixture-spec RFC documents MySQL engine coverage additively (no reinterpretation of existing fields).
engine.name = mysql is representable with its version, feeding version-conditional models.
Compatibility rules (RFC §12) are respected: unknown fields ignored, additive-only within major 1.
verification: spec review; a MySQL fixture validates against the schema and round-trips a stable digest

tasks.title: MySQL: pull / hydrate
tasks.description: Implement `pull` and `hydrate` for MySQL — the second engine (PRD §4). Read production shape read-only via the catalog + sampling, emit a MySQL fixture, and deterministically hydrate a disposable MySQL target. Reuse the confidence model and synthesis engine built for Postgres; only the catalog access and engine specifics differ.
acceptance_criteria: `pull` produces a valid MySQL fixture read-only, with confidence on every fact and privacy enforced.
`hydrate --seed N` deterministically reconstructs a disposable MySQL database honoring null_fraction, unique, and fan-out shape.
The synthesis engine and confidence model are shared with Postgres, not forked.
verification: go test ./... against a disposable MySQL; digest-stable pull; deterministic hydrate

tasks.title: MySQL: validate + MySQL-specific findings + corpus
tasks.description: Bring `validate` to MySQL with a MySQL corpus and MySQL-specific finding rules (online DDL semantics differ materially from Postgres). Corpus triples first (PRD §12). Findings render the same Verdict struct (PRD §10) — one contract across engines.
acceptance_criteria: A MySQL corpus runs (migration, fixture, expected verdict) triples against supported MySQL majors.
`validate` returns MySQL-appropriate findings via the shared Verdict struct.
Capping and bucketed estimates apply identically to MySQL (RFC §7.4, §9.2).
verification: MySQL corpus harness green; validate returns shared Verdict on MySQL targets

tasks.title: Cloud traction gate (build only if pull-through is real)
tasks.description: A decision checkpoint, not code: cloud does not start until the CLI has organic users (PRD §14 — a few hundred stars and unsolicited issues/PRs; PRD §14.1 week-20 kill criterion). Record the gate criteria, measure them, and only unblock P5-T8..T14 when they pass. Zero unsolicited issues/PRs is information, not a reason to push harder (PRD §14.1).
spec_refs: PRD §14 (cloud does not start until organic users)
PRD §9 (none of this gets built until §13 says so)
acceptance_criteria: The gate criteria (star threshold, unsolicited issues/PRs) are written down and measurable.
Cloud tasks P5-T8..T14 remain blocked until the gate is explicitly marked passed.
A negative gate result is recorded as a stop/reassess decision, not silently ignored.
verification: manual: gate criteria documented; cloud tasks reference this gate as a blocker

tasks.title: Cloud API — Go on Coolify + self-hosted Supabase, importing the CLI packages
tasks.description: Stand up the commercial API per PRD §9: Go, on Coolify, alongside self-hosted Supabase, importing the CLI's own `fixture` and `verdict` packages so canonical form, digesting, and verdict structs have exactly one implementation. A second (TypeScript) implementation would eventually disagree on float formatting or key ordering and attestations would silently stop verifying (PRD §9).
spec_refs: PRD §9 (Go API on Coolify + self-hosted Supabase; imports CLI fixture/verdict packages)
acceptance_criteria: The API is a Go service importing the CLI's `fixture` and `verdict` packages (no reimplemented canonicalization).
Deployed on Coolify with self-hosted Supabase (Postgres metadata, Storage for blobs, GoTrue auth).
`validate` never calls the cloud — the critical path never touches this infra (PRD §8.4/§9).
verification: API integration test: digest computed by API matches CLI digest for the same fixture

tasks.title: Fixture registry (the killer cloud feature)
tasks.description: Hosted, versioned, access-controlled fixtures (PRD §8.4): Dana pulls `prod@2026-07-14` without ever holding a prod credential — this is what gets `$PROD_URL` off her laptop. Fixtures are value-free by design (RFC §8), so a breach exposes statistics, not customer data, and they remain committed to git so the registry is a convenience, not a dependency (PRD §9).
spec_refs: PRD §9 (registry is a convenience, not a dependency)
acceptance_criteria: Fixtures can be pushed, versioned, and pulled by id (e.g. prod@2026-07-14) with access control.
A developer can pull a fixture without holding a production credential.
Registry downtime never breaks anyone's CI (fixtures still resolve from git).
verification: registry integration test: push/version/pull by id; access control enforced; git fallback works

tasks.title: Audit trail + approval gates
tasks.description: The compliance surface Priya writes the check for (PRD §8.4): an immutable audit trail of every validate — who, when, which fixture, what verdict — plus approval gates where a FAIL blocks the deploy and a WARN requires a named human override that is recorded. Never gate `validate` itself (PRD §8.4) — the moment validate is gated the agent loop breaks and the wedge dies.
spec_refs: PRD §8.4 (audit trail, approval gates; never gate validate)
acceptance_criteria: Every validate submitted to the cloud is recorded immutably (who/when/fixture/verdict).
Approval gates block a deploy on FAIL and require a recorded named override on WARN.
`validate` itself remains free and ungated — gating lives in the deploy gate, not the command.
verification: integration test: audit record immutable; FAIL blocks, WARN override recorded; validate never blocked

tasks.title: Drift detection (customer-side, signed results)
tasks.description: Scheduled `verify` against every environment, alerting when prod diverges from what the migration history claims (PRD §8.4). It MUST be customer-side (PRD §9): the customer runs a runner in their own infra that posts signed results — we never hold their production credentials, which would make us a breach target and kill the deal with the exact buyer we're chasing. The backend only receives and stores signed JSON.
spec_refs: PRD §9 (drift must be customer-side; receive and store signed JSON)
acceptance_criteria: A customer-side runner performs scheduled read-only `verify` and posts signed results.
The backend never holds production credentials — it only receives and stores signed JSON.
Divergence from the declared migration history raises an alert.
verification: integration test: signed verify result accepted and stored; unsigned/invalid rejected; no cred storage path exists

tasks.title: Agent attestation bound to CI workload identity
tasks.description: The differentiated feature (PRD §8.4): when an agent validated a migration, record which agent, which model, which fixture version, what verdict at the moment the PR opened. Self-reported identity is worthless — a `--agent=claude-code` flag is trivially spoofed — so attestation binds to a CI OIDC workload identity (GitHub Actions' token), signing verdict + fixture digest server-side. This makes attestation a CI product, not a laptop product; laptop validation stays useful and stays unattested.
spec_refs: PRD §8.4 (agent attestation; bind to CI OIDC, don't trust a flag)
PRD §9.1 (subject = fixture + migration digests; predicateType URI)
acceptance_criteria: Attestation binds to a CI OIDC workload identity, not a self-reported flag.
The signed predicate follows the PRD §9.1 shape (subject = fixture + migration digests, predicateType URI, verdict body as predicate).
Laptop validation is explicitly unattested and documented as such (the two are not blurred).
verification: integration test in CI: OIDC-bound attestation produced and verifies; spoofed flag alone yields no attestation

tasks.title: Attestation signing modes (Sigstore: public Rekor vs detached)
tasks.description: Resolve where signatures go (PRD §9.1 — decide in phase 5, not v1). Public Rekor makes immutability a property of the artifact, but keyless signing publishes repo identity + timing — free marketing for OSS, an unacceptable leak for a private enterprise monorepo. Ship two modes: public Rekor for OSS repos, and detached signatures held in the registry (or a private log) for enterprise. Don't be the root of trust — the artifact carries its own proof (PRD §9).
spec_refs: PRD §9.1 (two modes: public Rekor for OSS, detached/private for enterprise)
PRD §9 (don't be the root of trust)
acceptance_criteria: OSS mode publishes attestations to public Rekor, verifiable by anyone offline.
Enterprise mode uses detached signatures (registry or private log), leaking neither repo identity nor timing.
Verification works with zero trust in our server in both modes.
verification: test: OSS attestation verifies via Rekor; enterprise detached signature verifies offline without our server

tasks.title: Billing (Stripe) + pricing tiers — never gate validate
tasks.description: Wire billing per PRD §8.4/§9: Stripe, with the pricing shape ~$29/dev/mo or ~$99/connected-database/mo for registry + audit, enterprise for SSO/on-prem. The hard invariant: `validate` is free forever and never gated — the moment it's gated the agent loop breaks and the wedge dies (PRD §8.4). Cloud sells what only exists when there's more than one of you.
spec_refs: PRD §8.4 (pricing shape; never gate validate; MIT CLI, closed cloud)
acceptance_criteria: Stripe billing supports the per-dev and per-connected-database tiers plus an enterprise path.
`validate` (CLI + Action + MCP) remains free and ungated regardless of billing state.
The free CLI is complete on its own — a solo dev never hits a paywall (PRD §8.4).
verification: integration test: subscription lifecycle via Stripe test mode; validate works with no active subscription

milestones.note: Milestones map the PRD §14 sequence to shippable gates. The launch gate is the PRD §13 v1-done demo: a Claude Code session, given a repo and a fixture, told 'add a NOT NULL email column to users', produces a naive migration, watches it FAIL with RS-LOCK-001, rewrites it as a three-step backfill, and reaches PASS — with no human turn in between. Record it before writing the README.
milestones.launch_gate: PRD §13: the agent-loop demo closes with no human turn (naive migration -> FAIL RS-LOCK-001 -> three-step backfill -> PASS). That demo is the launch.

list.name: Foundation reserved, spec public, skeleton builds
list.definition: Namespaces + Go module path fixed; rowshape/fixture-spec public (RFC-0001 v1, MIT); single binary with stub subcommands; goreleaser signed + SBOM on five platforms; CI green.

list.name: pull + hydrate reproduce a corpus pathology (Week-6 gate)
list.definition: A read-only fast-mode `pull` emits a digest-stable <100KB fixture with confidence on every fact; `hydrate --seed N` deterministically reconstructs a disposable Postgres reproducing a known corpus pathology. The confidence engine (HLL, uniqueness probes, auto-escalation, inspect --leaks) is in.
list.kill_checkpoint: Week 6 (PRD §14.1): if pull + hydrate don't reproduce a known corpus pathology, the fixture premise is wrong — stop before building validate.

list.name: validate + verdict contract green across the PG matrix
list.definition: Corpus + capping tests land before the findings; one Verdict struct with two marshalers (DSSE-signable); `validate` returns RS-LOCK / RS-DATA / RS-CONSTRAINT / RS-INDEX with bucketed, version-conditional estimates and mandatory remediation, capped by confidence (RFC §7.4), across PG 11-17.

list.name: MCP server + init --agent wire the loop into the agent's turn
list.definition: `rowshape mcp` serves the four thin tools; `rowshape init --agent` writes MCP config, the versioned agent rule, and the pre-commit backstop so an agent reaches for rowshape unprompted.

list.name: Launch: agent-loop demo closes with no human turn (Week-12 gate)
list.definition: GitHub Action runs `validate` in CI rendering the shared Verdict; docs site live on Cloudflare Pages with the narrow-and-true privacy claim; the agent-loop demo runs end-to-end (naive migration -> FAIL RS-LOCK-001 -> three-step backfill -> PASS) with no human turn, guarded by a CI regression job. This is v1-done and the launch.
list.kill_checkpoint: Week 12 (PRD §14.1): if the demo doesn't work end-to-end without a human turn, the wedge isn't real — reassess.

list.name: Post-launch traction gate (Week-20) unlocks cloud
list.definition: Findings breadth (RS-PERF, RS-REVERSE), version-matrix depth, and MySQL proceed ungated. The cloud (registry, audit, drift, attestation, billing) is built only if the traction gate passes; `validate` is never gated.
list.kill_checkpoint: Week 20 (PRD §14.1): zero unsolicited issues/PRs is information, not a reason to push harder — cloud does not start without organic pull-through.

open_decisions.question: Capture pg_stat_statements in v1 fixtures without acting on it, or omit it entirely?
open_decisions.leaning: Capture, don't act (schema-additive; decide early because retrofitting the fixture schema is costly).

open_decisions.question: Disposable target: testcontainers-go (needs a Docker daemon, awkward on a cold agent machine) or embedded/pg_tmp (fewer prerequisites, more fragile)?
open_decisions.leaning: Unresolved — decides how frictionless the agent loop feels on first run; couples to the exit-3 tool-error path (P2-T17).

open_decisions.question: Does auto-escalation need a cost ceiling, and of what shape (--max-escalation-rows, timeout-degrades-to-omit, or prompt)?
open_decisions.leaning: Soft cap with a WARN naming what was skipped and why (implemented in P1b-T4).

open_decisions.question: HyperLogLog precision 14 (~1.6% error, 16KB state) vs 16 (~0.4%, 64KB)? The error rate is published in distinct.error and feeds §7.4 capping.
open_decisions.leaning: 14 — `measured` only needs to beat `estimated`, not approach `exact` (confirm in P1b-T1).

open_decisions.question: Partitioned tables: parent only, or every partition? Partition count and per-partition skew change lock behavior materially.
open_decisions.leaning: Parent declares partitions: {count, strategy, skew}; no per-partition entries (implemented in P1-T12).

open_decisions.question: Does bloat_estimate belong in a portable spec, or is it Postgres trivia that will embarrass us at MySQL?
open_decisions.leaning: Unresolved — revisit when MySQL vocabulary lands.

open_decisions.question: Multi-column correlation (country/phone_prefix; composite unique collisions understated by independent generation) — add a correlations block now or defer?
open_decisions.leaning: Defer to v2 — nonsense is usually fine (RFC §13); revisit only for composite unique constraints.
